content:
- Pixel Check-in - Fortnightly (Office Hours with Ramki Gaddipati)
- Structured Summary for Digital Twin

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This was a lengthy operations and governance check-in led by Ramki, focusing on Pixel card and Payzapp product lines, with the team presenting metrics, project updates, ongoing issues, and seeking clarity on directions. Ramki joined mid-flow and took charge, driving conversations on how product modules are structured, the need for better user content, and pushing for product thinking versus journey mapping. There was strong emphasis on deliverables, especially with respect to content production for user education, onboarding flows, repurposing/reusing technology stacks, web servicing, KPI reduction for customer support, and clear communication for both public/searchable and in-app content. Ramki challenged and redirected the team on a number of tactical and strategic items, focusing on information clarity, speed of execution, content accessibility, and the value of public-facing knowledge assets to reduce user pain and drive adoption. The session also discussed splitting operational review meetings by pillar, how to handle key blockers (like recurring UPI), and project management clarity on ownership, deadlines, and scope.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Product Module Re-Definition:
- Ramki directed a mindshift from linear "user journeys" to defining Pixel/Payzapp as a set of independently usable product modules, each with entry and exit points, to break product complexity and boost reusability.
(Emphasis: "Tell me what your modules are, each of them...should be independently usable.")

- Content Production as Strategic Priority:
- Heavy push by Ramki to produce weekly, public-facing and indexable 'how-to' videos and documentation, ensuring accessibility for bots/search engines as well as direct user support.
(Emphasis on content being non-marketing, strictly help/how-to, and "cheap to produce.")

- Target of Sub-1% Contact Rate:
- Clear operational mandate for CS teams: all post-issuance reporting should be focused on tracking and reducing contact rate to below 1%, not just sharing absolute figures.
(Mandate: “I want contact rate to be sub 1%, is that clear?”)

- Clarity/Transparency in Project Status Reporting:
- Ramki required project tracking formats to prioritize projected 'prod' release dates, clear up ambiguous language (definition vs. delivery), and make milestones and progress/cadence easily trackable for all.
(Call for clarity in dashboard/reporting columns around status, delivery, and definition.)

- Web Servicing as Accelerated Deliverable:
- Strong push to rapidly define and deliver B1 version of Pixel/Payzapp web servicing UI by leveraging reusable services/components, with CTO office sponsorship and fixed resource allocation.
(Stated: “If we have a definition, it will be done in less than a month.”)

- Shift Meeting Structure for Operational Governance:
- Instruction to break up the current catch-all meetings into more focused operational check-ins: manufacturing governance, product performance, and CS, citing inefficiency and lack of depth when all issues are discussed together.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Modular Product Architecture:
- Ramki insisted that product teams stop organizing around “journeys” and instead define clear, reusable modules for all major Pixel/Payzapp features. He emphasized each module should have independent entry/exit criteria and be directly launchable, rather than tightly sequenced in a user journey. He was critical of the current “linear” thinking and demanded a fundamental reframe for product/content architecture, seeking resilience and flexibility. His tone was assertive, almost impatient, and he repeatedly pressed teams to validate modularity—less talk, more demonstration.

2. Prioritization and Cheap, Effective Content Production:
- Ramki treated content as a core product deliverable, arguing that user and bot-accessible how-to assets (“public content”) should be produced weekly and made discoverable to reduce customer pain and product confusion. He described high-velocity, low-cost ways to generate such video and documentation assets, dismissing internal hurdles and pushing teams to “keep producing”—reducing over-analysis, approval fears, or over-indexing on in-app comms. He valued content’s impact on scaling user support above iterative UI fixes for user guidance.

3. Operational Excellence and Metrics Focus:
- Ramki was highly metric-driven, setting an explicit sub-1% contact rate as a critical North Star for CS/support teams. He critiqued teams for focusing on absolute numbers rather than deltas or progress towards strategic targets, and mandated reporting frameworks equip him to question both product and engineering functions on their contribution to this KPI.

4. Project Tracking Discipline and Transparency:
- He flagged and sought to eliminate ambiguity in project updates—especially around “definitions,” delivery milestones, or UAT/prod cadences. He wanted status dashboards that prioritize what is most valuable (“is it in prod, or when will it be”), and called teams to adopt more standardized, less ambiguous updates. He saw current reporting as potentially misleading, stalling productive escalation and decision making.

5. Accelerated Web Productization:
- Ramki made a forceful case for delivering a trimmed, functional B1 web servicing product (for Pixel/Payzapp), leveraging existing services/components, sponsored by the CTO office. He rejected the need for deep re-definition, and instead, advocated for demonstration via quick, disciplined delivery—not isolated design. He positioned this as a strategic enabler for bank channel independence and architectural re-use.

6. Content as Strategic Differentiator (Not Marketing):
- Ramki specifically distinguished between informational content and product marketing, directing all effort to “how-to,” factual, support content. He wants public knowledge bases, YouTube/channel assets, and indexed help that bots and end-users can consume—prioritizing reach and discoverability over design, branding, or feature splash. He described content as a scalable, immediate lever for both product success and reduced support costs.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Accelerate Content Production for Support/Education
- Task: Initiate ongoing, weekly production of easily consumable, public-facing videos and documentation (how-to’s, FAQ, module guides) for Pixel and Payzapp (across channels: YouTube, help center, web).
- Assigned To: Product, CS, and Content/Comms teams (Sharath P as primary coordinator; Rekha for approvals)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology:
- Not for product marketing—focus on actual user anxieties/problems; prioritize “how to” and DIY fixes.
- Make content easy for bots/search indexes (public, not just in-app); translate into major Indian languages for accessibility.
- Use low-cost production methods (bot voiceover, agent walkthroughs, screen captures).
- Don’t delay for approvals—show working assets and iterate.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Begin immediately, with one new content unit per week, cadence plan to be submitted to Ramki for review/approval.

2. Redefine Product Reporting, Metrics and Governance
- Task: Revise reporting/dashboards to focus on KPI progress (esp. sub-1% contact rate), showing delta month-on-month and clear path to target. Redefine project status updates for clarity (prod dates, clarity in columns).
- Assigned To: CS team, Product Ops, all data/metrics owners.
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology:
- Rework the check-in decks—every post-issuance metric should reflect progress to 1%; not just absolute numbers.
- Project dashboards should prioritize prod release dates; clarify ambiguous columns/statuses.
- All recurring/operational reviews to be split by topic/pillar for deeper focus.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediate revision expected; new reporting to be presented at next operational check-in.

3. Deliver B1 Web Servicing (Pixel/Payzapp)
- Task: Rapidly define and build first (minimal, working) version of Pixel/Payzapp servicing UI for web, usable across relevant bank channels.
- Assigned To: Manish Sharma (with support from Apurva, Sharath P, and two dedicated resources from CTO office).
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology:
- Definition and build NOT to be done in isolation—leverage reusable Flutter components, align with current services; keep scope to core servicing.
- No commitment to banks until internal demo is ready.
- Target is working demo/product, not documentation.
- Multiple products to demo (passbook, rewards, cards, etc.)
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Definition and B1 delivery in under one month.

4. Investigate/Resolve UPI Recurring/Autopay Integration
- Task: Clarify and accelerate solution for supporting recurring UPI mandates/transactions for Pixel.
- Assigned To: Navakanth Kanapala, Nikhil, relevant product/engineering leads.
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology:
- Identify exact blockers; bring solution to regular check-ins; ensure end-to-end coverage for Pixel; keep parity with Payzapp/Pay Later/Prepaid.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Actionable solution/findings by next reporting cycle.

5. Prepare Content/Channel Plan for Ramki Sign-Off
- Task: Submit a concise (1-pager/2-paragraph) plan outlining weekly content cadence, targeted channels, and content priorities for Ramki’s approval.
- Assigned To: Sharath P
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology:
- Keep it brief and clear; prioritize actionable, not theoretical or marketing content.
- Only after Ramki’s review should approval with Rekha/bank be sought.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediately; prior to bank or external pitches.

- VII. Jira Ticket

Scope:
- Weekly public content production for Pixel/Payzapp how-to and support assets (YouTube, help center, web, translation).
- B1 Web servicing UI delivery for Pixel/Payzapp, leveraging existing backend/services and Flutter stack; definition and delivery in <1 month.
- Dashboard/reporting update to focus on KPI delta/sub-1% contact rate for operations and support; format improvements for project status decks.

Assignee:
- Content ticket: Sharath P (+ cross-functional support from CS, product, content teams)
- Web UI ticket: Manish Sharma (w/ two resources provided by CTO office)
- Dashboard/reporting ticket: CS metrics/data leads (Zeta CS/Arvind)

Justification:
- These items represent high-priority, time-bound deliverables central to product learning, operational excellence, and governance visibility, as defined by Ramki.
- Content and web app items are both immediately actionable and sponsored at CTO-level, earmarked for rapid iteration.
- Reporting/dashboard revision is a recurring, non-engineering item but critical for operational steering.
- UPI/recurring work likely tracked in separate technical epics due to cross-product impact and is not scoped for Jira as an independent ticket without further solutioning.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Sharath P/team to deliver a 1-pager outlining content cadence/channels for Ramki’s alignment prior to bank/external discussion.
- CS/Product teams to rework reporting with clear progression toward sub-1% contact rate and adjust deck structure ahead of next operational review.
- Manish Sharma and technical team to prioritize web servicing demo readiness, leveraging only essential additions for definition, and to coordinate directly with CTO office for accelerated execution.
- Product/Engineering leads to bring a consolidated update on UPI recurring mandate support status, blockers, and next steps for cross-product coverage at the next cycle.
- All teams to ensure public documentation/content produced is accessible, discoverable, and translated as per Ramki’s directive, with rapid iteration and minimal approval friction.

- If clarification is needed on any instruction or status, teams are urged to summarize and escalate with specifics, as Ramki emphasizes reduction in ambiguity and over-analysis in both content and operational processes.

attendees: Ramki Gaddipati (joins as "Pink Floyd" in transcript)
